expectations.text: Produces a standalone review-report document that follows the prescribed structure exactly: a Summary section, an 'Orphan changes' section, a 'Scenario reviews' section with one subsection per scenario, a 'Missed corner cases' section, and a 'Recommended actions' section.
expectations.evidence: Report uses the required headings: Summary, Orphan changes (not traceable to any scenario), Scenario reviews, Missed corner cases, and Recommended actions.

expectations.text: The Summary does a coverage check stating roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented and explicitly flags S3 (idempotent retry), S4 (ownership authorization), and S5 (assembly-failure terminal state) as unimplemented / having no associated code changes.
expectations.evidence: Summary states "Coverage: 2 of 5 scenarios have code changes attached" and explicitly lists S3, S4, and S5 as unimplemented.

expectations.text: Detects the usage_analytics module (PostHog tracking + active_creators_daily rollup) as an orphan change that maps to no scenario in the blueprint, and calls it out as unrequested scope creep that should not be there.
expectations.evidence: Orphan section calls out `backend/app/services/usage_analytics.py` and inline `track_export(...)` as unrequested analytics scope creep.

expectations.text: For EACH scenario the review argues why the implementation is unacceptable with concrete reasons (adversarial framing), rather than praising or signing anything off; unimplemented scenarios are argued as unacceptable because nothing was built.
expectations.evidence: Each S1-S5 subsection has a "Why the implementation is unacceptable" bullet; S3-S5 are treated as unacceptable because they are unimplemented.

expectations.text: Cites specific kite-arch-compass principles behind the findings, including the inline zip assembly + DB access in the route handler violating thin-edges (Principle 2), layering (Principle 1), and do-slow-work-asynchronously (Principle 19); the missing idempotency for S3 (Principle 15); the missing server-side ownership check trusting a client-supplied workspace_id for S4 (least privilege Principle 27 and/or never-trust-input Principle 29); and the absent failed terminal state / non-resumable flow for S5 (Principle 16 and/or Principle 21).
expectations.evidence: Principle sections cite P1/P2/P19 for inline route assembly, P15 for S3 idempotency, P27/P28/P29 for S4 authorization/input trust, and P16/P21 for S5 terminal-state failures.

expectations.text: Flags that the S2 download endpoint streams the artifact with no status==ready gate, so a failed or half-written export could be served as a valid download.
expectations.evidence: S2 review says the endpoint "never verifies `status == ready`" and cannot distinguish ready/downloaded/failed exports.

expectations.text: For at least the implemented scenarios, states the concrete impact ('because this is wrong, here is the consequence') AND writes a failure scenario as Gherkin (given/when/then) describing an input under which the implementation actually breaks.
expectations.evidence: S1 and S2 include fenced Gherkin failure scenarios and explicit impact bullets.

expectations.text: Hunts missed corner cases BEYOND those the blueprint listed (Step 9) — e.g. app files changing mid-assembly, very large apps blocking the request thread / memory, or export-artifact retention and cleanup — not merely restating the blueprint's own S3-S5 corner cases.
expectations.evidence: Missed corner cases include file mutation during assembly, large export memory pressure, and artifact retention/cleanup.

expectations.text: Does NOT fix the code, rewrite the implementation, or re-trigger planning/implementation; any remediation appears only as advice under 'Recommended actions'.
expectations.evidence: The output is only a review report; remediation is advice under Recommended actions and no implementation files were changed by the child.

expectations.text: Maps each chunk of the diff to the scenario it serves and reports coverage as roughly 2 of 5 scenarios implemented, explicitly naming S3, S4, and S5 as unimplemented.
expectations.evidence: Summary reports 2 of 5 scenarios with associated code changes and names S3, S4, and S5 as unimplemented; sections map route, migration, download, idempotency, auth, failure, and analytics changes.

expectations.text: Identifies the usage_analytics module as an orphan change traceable to no scenario and recommends it not be there (scope creep).
expectations.evidence: Orphan changes section identifies `usage_analytics.py` and inline `track_export(...)` as unrequested PostHog/rollup scope creep.

expectations.text: Argues, scenario by scenario, why each implementation is unacceptable rather than assessing whether it is good, holding the adversarial stance even where code superficially looks fine.
expectations.evidence: Each S1-S5 Scenario reviews subsection explains why the implementation is unacceptable rather than praising it.

expectations.text: Cites specific kite-arch-compass principle numbers/names for the violations (e.g. Principle 2 thin edges and Principle 19 async for the inline assembly in the route; Principle 15 idempotency for S3; least-privilege / never-trust-input for the missing S4 ownership check; Principle 16 / Principle 21 for S5's missing failed terminal state).
expectations.evidence: Principle citations include P1/P2/P19 for route assembly, P15 for idempotency, P27/P29/P28 for authorization/input trust, and P16/P21 for failure state/retries.

expectations.text: States the consequence/impact of each major defect and writes at least one concrete failure case as Gherkin (given/when/then) where the implementation breaks (e.g. a retried POST creating duplicate exports, or a cross-workspace export succeeding).
expectations.evidence: Each major scenario states impact and includes a fenced Gherkin failure scenario, including retry duplicate and cross-workspace export cases.

expectations.text: Surfaces at least one corner case the blueprint did not list (e.g. concurrent file mutation during assembly, unbounded memory on large apps, or stale/orphaned export artifacts).
expectations.evidence: Missed corner cases include path traversal, file mutation during assembly, artifact overwrite, large export memory pressure, missing row/file handling, and analytics provider failure.

expectations.text: Delivers the findings as a review report for a human to decide on and does not itself fix the code or re-run the implementation/planning pipeline.
expectations.evidence: The output is a review report only; Recommended actions are advisory and no implementation/planning run is triggered.

expectations.text: Resists the 'sign off / give it the green light / merge today' framing and does NOT approve or bless the feature; it delivers an adversarial review report whose verdict is that the feature is not ready.
expectations.evidence: Summary says 2 of 5 scenarios have code changes but 0 of 5 satisfy the blueprint/design, and it does not approve the feature.

expectations.text: Does not treat the plan's 'committed' status for S1/S2 as proof they work — it checks the actual diff and argues the implemented scenarios are themselves defective, not merely the unbuilt ones.
expectations.evidence: The S1/S2 sections inspect the actual diff and call out defects despite the prompt and plan claiming they are committed/working.

expectations.evidence: Principle citations include P1/P2/P19, P15, P27/P28/P29, and P16/P21 as required.

notes: Iteration 1: with_skill passed all 25 expectations across 3 evals; without_skill passed 17/25. The +0.32 delta is driven by exact report structure, principle citations, concrete Gherkin failure cases, and missed-corner-case coverage.

Baselines found many real defects, so the suite is not merely testing generic bug-finding. The gap is that baselines usually produced merge-review prose rather than the prescribed final Kite feature-review artifact.

The skilled runs spent substantially more time and tokens, especially eval 2. Transcript review shows the skill drove broad architecture-reference loading; the skill was revised to make kite-arch-compass lookup more targeted.
The original Fan out instruction conflicts with benchmark/resource-constrained execution. The skill was revised to make fan-out conditional while preserving one-scenario-at-a-time isolation.
